critique.summary: The article reports that several newly created accounts on the Polymarket prediction platform placed large bets on a US-Iran ceasefire, raising suspicions of insider knowledge or insider trading. Experts and observers note patterns such as wallet splitting and timing of bets that suggest some bettors may have had access to non-public information.
mainPoints: Eight new accounts created around 21 March bet nearly $70,000 on a US-Iran ceasefire before 31 March, potentially winning up to $820,000.
An account created before the US strike on Iran on 28 February also made a successful bet on that event, raising previous insider trading concerns.
Experts highlight wallet splitting and timing of bets as signs of insider knowledge or attempts to conceal identity.
Polymarket's probability rating for a ceasefire increased from 6% to 24% in a few days, with over $21 million wagered on this outcome.
Prediction markets like Polymarket are increasingly used in geopolitical conflicts, with allegations of war profiteering and insider trading.
Polymarket has faced regulatory scrutiny and criticism, including for spreading misinformation on its social media.
The bet requires official public confirmation from both US and Iranian governments to be settled as a ceasefire.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims about suspicious betting patterns on Polymarket are consistent with publicly available information about the platform and its use in geopolitical prediction markets. The reported dates, amounts wagered, and expert opinions align with known features of crypto-based prediction markets and previous reports of similar insider trading concerns. However, the article relies heavily on expert speculation and anonymous sources, and Polymarket itself has not provided a direct response, so definitive proof of insider trading is not established. The regulatory scrutiny and criticism of Polymarket for misinformation and war profiteering are documented in other reputable sources, supporting the article's assertions.
considerations: The anonymity of crypto wallets makes it difficult to conclusively prove insider trading or identify bettors.
Prediction markets reflect probabilities but do not guarantee outcomes; insider knowledge may not be sufficient to win bets requiring official confirmation.
The article does not provide perspectives from Polymarket representatives or regulatory authorities.
The broader ethical and legal implications of using prediction markets for geopolitical events are not deeply explored.
Potential market manipulation or misinformation campaigns on social media related to these bets warrant further investigation.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the credibility of insider knowledge claims related to bets on a US-Iran ceasefire, with skepticism about the significance of the bets and the reliability of the sources. Several comments critique the political context, the role of Trump, and the nature of prediction markets, while others question the article's sensationalism and lack of evidence.

comments.author: boramalper
comments.text:
> The wallets “definitely [look like] someone with some degree of inside info”, said Ben Yorke, formerly a researcher with CoinTelegraph, now building an AI trading platform called Starchild.

Ben Yorke is the only expert I see mentioned in the article, so it'd be a lot more accurate (and a lot less sensational) if The Guardian changed its title to "... says one expert" (but it wouldn't sound as interesting then, would it?).

> Eight accounts, all newly created around 21 March, bet a total of nearly $70,000 (£52,000) on there being a ceasefire. They stand to make nearly $820,000 if such a deal is reached before 31 March.

Not to sound privileged but $800k doesn't sound like that much of money for someone that has access to that kind of insider knowledge, especially considering the risks.

All things considered, I feel like the same people could make much bigger bets using trad-fi instruments than Polymarket so I don't understand what's so significant about Polymarket "whales".

In the end people just betting on TACO.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a measured critique of the article's sensationalism and questions the significance of the bets, highlighting the need for more nuanced reporting.

comments.author: tmnvix
comments.text:
> “For the purposes of this market, an ‘official ceasefire agreement’ requires clear public confirmation from both the United States government and the government of Iran that they have agreed to halt military hostilities against one another.” (by March 31)

Seems a rather risky bet considering how deep Israel has ventured into its war in Lebanon. Very doubtful they will stop anytime soon. Given the leverage Israel has demonstrated over US foreign policy I find it hard to imagine that the US will 'leave them to it'. Likewise, Iran is unlikely to leave Hezbollah's interests out of any negotiations or for that matter to trust that the US isn't asking for negotiations in bad faith. I guess there could be a limited ceasefire agreed between Iran and the US to make room for negotiations but the ceasefire would almost certainly have to occur without the opening of the strait or an end to fighting in Lebanon. This (now regional) war has a long way to go in my opinion.

Ultimately, I think this all hinges on whether or not the Iranians feel that they have enough leverage to exercise yet. My feeling is that they will want to continue to the point of destroying Trump's political career - something they could possibly do if this quagmire continues to get worse and closer to the US midterms. Bringing down a US president is potentially one way they can help ensure that they don't simply get attacked again in the near future.
comments.analysis: Offers a well-reasoned geopolitical analysis that contextualizes the ceasefire bet within broader regional conflicts, adding depth to the discussion.

comments.author: bjackman
comments.text:
What signs? This article is absolute slop (not AI, but slop).

I do not have any reason to doubt that people are doing insider trading. The US admin is obviously corrupt and the Iran attacks are the most abject symptom of its corruption so far.

But you can't just put out a bunch of completely isolated observations with zero analysis and say "that looks like insider trading". There is nothing at all in here that presents an argument for that claim.

I am a daily Guardian reader but I stopped paying for it coz there are so many articles like this that are just complete fucking trash. Because I am the target audience (leftist Euro who can easily get riled up by topics like this) it pisses me off when I feel I'm being manipulated.
comments.analysis: Critiques the article's methodology and calls for stronger evidence, emphasizing the importance of rigorous analysis in such claims.

comments.author: jmyeet
comments.text:
There are lists of the worst decisions of the Supreme Court in history (eg [1]). Dred Scott [2] often tops such lists. I believe that Trump v. United States [3] will go on such lists in the near future. Why? Because it opened the floodgates on corruption on a scale we previously haven't seen. The president has absolute immunity. The president's communications with the Attorney-General can't even be examined, basically. And there was absolutely no constitutional basis for it. The Court established a King.

So the president has absolute immunity from any consequences. The president can pardon anyone in their orbit. Pardons are now being openly sold for personal profit [4].

All of this was completely foreseeable from giving someone absolute immunity.

Prediction markets make this much worse because they're even more unregulated. We certainly had corruption even in Trump's first term (eg Jared Kushner's Saudi "investment" [5]). This is the new normal.

[1]: https://www.findlaw.com/legalblogs/supreme-court/13-worst-su...

[2]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dred_Scott_v._Sandford

[3]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trump_v._United_States

[4]: https://www.cato.org/blog/embarrassment-riches

[5]: https://www.bbc.com/news/world-us-canada-68296877
comments.analysis: Provides historical and legal context linking political immunity and corruption to the risks of unregulated prediction markets, enriching the debate.

comments.author: 871ajh-121
comments.text:
Trump always announces ceasefires, negotiations and "deals" when the other party doesn't even know about it. Or, in the middle of real negotiations, he attacks.

This 5 day extension is to replenish US stocks and build up the marines in the region. while Israel continues to attack all the time.

The only deal that may have emerged in secret is for Iran not to hit the Dimona nuclear power plant and for Israel/US not to hit the Bushehr nuclear power plant.

Of course Trump's cronies will utilize the volatility and insider knowledge. The entire presidency is there to enrich his clan.
comments.analysis: Highlights the complexity of ceasefire announcements and suggests strategic motives, though some claims about insider knowledge are speculative.

comments.author: b112
comments.text:
This is very weird.

Trump is the ultimate mercurial. At the drop of a hat, he'll change his mind on things. Predicting Trump is a ridiculous proposition, and I'm willing to be most would agree with that.

Even Trump doesn't know what Trump will do next!

Yet meanwhile, there are people using "insider knowledge" to make decisions? What insider knowledge!! There is none. It's all made up on a day to day basis.
comments.analysis: Points out the difficulty of predicting actions in a volatile political environment, questioning the premise of insider knowledge claims.

comments.author: broof
comments.text: Isn’t one of the points of betting markets is to incentivize this? Now me, as someone who doesn’t have access to insider knowledge, can know that there’s a noticeably increased chance of what’s going to happen. I do think maybe there should be some sort of feature to prevent last minute bets that utilizes insider knowledge, and I do admit there are considerable negative externalities to this system but it’s not clear to me that this is for sure bad.
comments.analysis: Offers a balanced view on the role of prediction markets and insider knowledge, proposing practical improvements.

comments.author: Insanity
comments.text: Highly skeptical of using a betting platform as an indication of insider knowledge and something to base your decisions off of. People will bet, and it's a 50/50 decision between "ceasfire yes / no". People vote with their gut.
comments.analysis: Provides a cautionary perspective on interpreting betting market data, emphasizing randomness and human psychology.

comments.author: mikkupikku
comments.text: If the administration decides to end this stupid war just so they can cash out some profitable bets... I don't even know what I would think.  The whole thing is so stupid it makes my head hurt.
comments.analysis: Expresses frustration without evidence or constructive insight, reflecting emotional response rather than analysis.

comments.author: ck2
comments.text:
There's no ceasefire, Iran has no idea what he is making up as always.

He's delaying until the Marines get there in a few days.

And until the markets close on Friday, notice how he said 5 days on Monday.

He's also bombed them the past two times in the middle of negotiations, why stop now

The man is a proven constant pathological liar since the day he came down the golden escalator 10 years ago, literally every time he speaks it's a lie.
comments.analysis: While some claims are consistent with known patterns, the tone is highly emotional and accusatory, reducing analytical value.
